content:
# 1 Corinthians Chapter 4 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1CO_004_page_001.jpg

1. Thus let a man consider us as servants of Christos and stewards of [the] mysteries of God.

2. Here, moreover, it is required in the stewards that one be found faithful.

3. But to me it is a smallest [thing] that I be judged by you or by a human day; but neither do I judge myself.

4. For I am conscious of nothing against myself, but I am not justified by this; but the [one] judging me is [the] Lord.

5. Therefore do not judge anything before [the] time, until the Lord comes, who will both bring to light the hidden [things] of the darkness and reveal the purposes of the hearts; and then the praise will come to each from God.

6. And these [things], brothers, I applied to myself and Apollos for your sake, that in us you may learn the [saying] "not beyond what has been written," that you may not be puffed up, one on behalf of the one against the other.

7. For who distinguishes you? And what do you have that you did not receive? And if indeed you received [it], why do you boast as not having received?

8. Already you are filled! Already you became rich! Apart from us you reigned! And I wish indeed you did reign, that we also might reign with you.

9. For I think that God displayed us the apostles last, as condemned to death, because we became a spectacle to the world, both to angels and to men.

10. We [are] fools on account of Christos, but you [are] wise in Christos; we [are] weak, but you [are] strong; you [are] honored, but we [are] dishonored.

11. Until the present hour we both hunger and thirst and are poorly clothed and are beaten and are homeless,

12. and we labor, working with the own hands of us. Being reviled, we bless; being persecuted, we endure;

13. being slandered, we encourage. As refuse of the world we became, scum of all [things], until now.

14. Not shaming you I write these [things], but as beloved children of me I admonish [you].

15. For if you should have ten thousand tutors in Christos, yet not many fathers; for in Christos Yeshua through the good news I fathered you.

16. Therefore I urge you, become imitators of me.

17. For this [reason] I sent to you Timotheos, who is my beloved and faithful child in [the] Lord, who will remind you of the ways of me, the [ones] in Christos, just as everywhere in every assembly I teach.

18. But some were puffed up, as [if] I were not coming to you.

19. But I will come to you quickly, if the Lord wills, and I will know not the speech of the [ones] having been puffed up but the power.

20. For the kingdom of God [is] not in speech but in power.

21. What do you wish? With a rod shall I come to you, or in love and a spirit of gentleness?

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.6 — "not beyond what has been written":** τὸ μὴ ὑπὲρ ἃ γέγραπται — this obscure phrase may be a Corinthian slogan or a marginal gloss that entered the text. It likely refers to Scripture generally: do not go beyond what Scripture teaches about boasting.

2. **v.9 — "spectacle" (θέατρον):** The image is of the arena — gladiators condemned to die as public entertainment. Paul and the apostles are the last act in the cosmic amphitheater, watched by the universe.

3. **v.13 — "refuse...scum":** περικαθάρματα, περίψημα — extremely strong terms. The first refers to the scrapings from a pot; the second to ritual scapegoats or human sacrifices offered to avert plague. Paul identifies with the lowest of society.

4. **v.15 — "ten thousand tutors":** μυρίους παιδαγωγούς — the παιδαγωγός was a slave who escorted children to school. Paul contrasts the many tutors with the one father. He alone gave the Corinthians spiritual birth.

5. **v.21 — "rod or love":** The chapter ends with a stark choice. Paul's fatherly authority can be exercised either way. The Corinthians' response will determine which.

— EMET, AI Translator
